title: Optomechanically controlled response amplification for enhanced quantum sensing
abstract: We show that strongly amplified dynamical responses in cavity optomechanical systems can be harnessed for enhanced quantum sensing. By tuning the optomechanical interaction to a regime of enhanced susceptibility, weak perturbations produce disproportionately large changes in the system response, leading to substantially improved estimation precision. Using Gaussian estimation theory, we demonstrate that the quantum Fisher information exhibits a divergent scaling as the perturbation strength decreases, implying a corresponding suppression of the estimation error. We further show that heterodyne detection of the output cavity field yields the classical Fisher information with the same asymptotic scaling as the quantum Fisher information, demonstrating that the enhanced sensitivity is accessible with a standard measurement protocol. These results identify amplified optomechanical dynamics as a controllable resource for quantum enhanced sensing and metrology.
summary: The paper develops a theoretical analysis showing that cavity optomechanical systems can act as highly sensitive quantum sensors. By tuning the system parameters to induce an effective dynamical singularity, the estimation precision (quantified by QFI) diverges as the perturbation strength decreases. This demonstrates a controllable mechanism for achieving enhanced metrology using standard measurement techniques.
main_problem: The research aims to harness strongly amplified dynamical responses in cavity optomechanical systems for achieving enhanced quantum sensing and metrology.
main_result: By tuning the system near a dynamically induced singularity, the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) exhibits divergent scaling with decreasing perturbation strength, leading to significantly suppressed estimation error.
method: The analysis employs Gaussian estimation theory, utilizing input-output formalism in the frequency domain and solving for Green's functions to quantify the sensitivity via QFI and CFI.
model_or_system: The system is a cavity optomechanical setup where an optical mode couples to a mechanical oscillator via radiation pressure. The dynamics are modeled using linearized quantum Langevin equations derived from the full Hamiltonian in the rotating frame.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), Classical Fisher Information (CFI), and the output field covariance matrix ($\mathbf{V}_{	ext{out}}^F[\omega]$).
important_parameters: The system is analyzed by tuning parameters to achieve a singular regime where $\det \mathcal{H}_{	ext{eff}} = 0$, which maximizes sensitivity.
main_assumptions: Linearization of dynamics around mean fields, use of Gaussian estimation theory, and assuming the probe field noise can be treated via input-output formalism.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate the setup (optics coupled to mechanics) and show comparisons between scaling behaviors ($\delta^*	heta$ vs. $	heta$) in both singular (enhanced sensitivity) and non-singular regimes (saturation).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the physical system and deriving linearized equations of motion, moving into the frequency domain analysis using scattering matrices to determine output field statistics, culminating in the application of quantum estimation theory to identify optimal sensing regimes.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete theoretical framework demonstrating how engineered nonlinear coupling (optomechanics) can be used to actively control system dynamics toward singular points that enhance fundamental measurement limits beyond standard scaling laws.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the cavity optomechanical system. The setup consists of an optical cavity mode ˆa coupled via radiation pressure to a mechanical (phonon) mode ˆb associated with the movable cavity element. The optical probe channel ˆF in(out) a , coupled with strength κa, represents the experimentally accessible input (output) field used for parameter estimation. The remaining channels, namely ˆF in(out) b and the bath fields ˆLin(out) a,b , with coupling rates κb and ηa,b, are ficti- tious/uncontrollable inaccessible channels introduced within the ef- fective input–output description of the system.

(56) is tuned to a singular configuration using system parameter given in Eq. (63) along with the control variable α, β and probe frequency ω. Both the quantum and classical bounds exhibit the quadratic scaling δ∗θ ∝θ2, in agreement with the analytical predictions of Eqs. FIG. 3. Schematic of heterodyne detection via dual balanced ho- modyne measurements. The probe output mode is first combined with vacuum at a beam splitter, generating two output ports. Each port is subsequently measured independently using balanced homo- dyne detection. The first output is combined with a local oscillator LO1 on a second beam splitter, and the resulting outputs are mea- sured by detectors D1 and D2, which access the quadrature qk. The second output is combined with a local oscillator LO2 on another beam splitter, with detection performed by D3 and D4, which mea- sure the quadrature pk.
